perturbation.original_text: that this agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of England and Wales and the courts of England and Wales shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims)
perturbation.changed_text: that this agreement shall be governed by the principles of equity and fairness, and any disputes shall be resolved through mediation in a neutral venue of the parties' choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from the specific laws of England and Wales to 'principles of equity and fairness' creates ambiguity. While equitable principles exist within the English legal system, this phrasing removes the certainty of statutory and case law, making enforcement unpredictable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Lack of Specified Governing Law under English Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the need for a clearly defined governing law, as the 'principles of equity and fairness' are subjective and open to interpretation, differing from established legal precedent under English law. The Act says which law is applicable when a contract doesn't specify. The changed text obscures this.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the law of England and Wales governs the agreement, ensuring clarity and predictability. The modified text replaces this with 'principles of equity and fairness,' which are subjective and lack the defined legal framework of statutory and case law. While equity exists within the English legal system, relying solely on it removes the certainty of established legal precedent. The scraped law snippet 1 shows the range of UK legislation, emphasizing the defined nature of legal acts and instruments. The modified text deviates from this defined framework, creating a contradiction because it introduces ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the contract under a specific legal jurisdiction, as it replaces it with a less defined standard. This misalignment with a defined legal system constitutes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: that any notices under this agreement shall be sent by first-class prepaid post to the address of the recipient party as set out in this letter and shall be deemed to have been received on the second week day (that is, any day excluding Sundays or public or bank holidays in England) following the day on which it was posted.
perturbation.changed_text: that any notices under this agreement shall be sent by electronic mail to the address of the recipient party and shall be deemed to have been received instantaneously upon sending, irrespective of actual receipt.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the method of notice from first-class prepaid post to electronic mail and deeming it received instantaneously contradicts established legal precedent in England and Wales regarding the service of notices, especially considering issues of deliverability and proof of service.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Requirements for Service of Notices under English Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: The Law of Property Act 1925, s 196
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the common law principle that service of notices must be properly effected, including consideration of the timing and proof of delivery. Instantaneous receipt cannot be guaranteed with electronic mail. Section 196 (4) defines what is properly served.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the method of notice from first-class prepaid post to electronic mail and deems it received instantaneously upon sending, irrespective of actual receipt. This contradicts established legal precedent, particularly in England and Wales, concerning the service of notices, including aspects of deliverability and proof of service. The Law Explanation suggests a contradiction with the common law principle of properly effected service of notices, addressing timing and proof of delivery. While the snippets do not explicitly state the exact method of delivery that is required, the general principle of needing to properly serve is still valid, thus this deviation creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: that the provisions of this agreement shall last for the duration of the discussions relating to the Proposed Transaction and for 2 years following their conclusion.
perturbation.changed_text: that the provisions of this agreement shall last indefinitely, regardless of the conclusion of discussions relating to the Proposed Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the time limitation on the confidentiality provisions from 2 years to 'indefinitely' can be considered unreasonable restraint of trade under English law, particularly if it unduly restricts the other party's business activities after the deal falls through.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restraint of Trade Doctrine under English Law
perturbation.law_citation: Nordenfelt v Maxim Nordenfelt Guns and Ammunition Co [1894] AC 535
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the legal principle that restraints of trade must be reasonable in duration and scope to be enforceable. An indefinite restraint is likely to be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, particularly if it significantly hinders legitimate business activities. Nordenfelt sets out the scope of reasonable restraint.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the duration of the confidentiality agreement from a specific period (2 years after discussions conclude) to 'indefinitely.' The Law Explanation states that 'restraints of trade must be reasonable in duration and scope to be enforceable' and that 'an indefinite restraint is likely to be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable.' The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that removing the time limitation could be considered unreasonable restraint of trade. The original text provided a clear time limit, while the changed text removes this limitation altogether, creating a potentially unreasonable and unenforceable restraint, thus contradicting the legal principle.

perturbation.original_text: that these terms shall not apply where the Confidential Information: 7.1 was known to us (by means not importing any requirement of confidentiality or involving a breach of confidentiality by a third party) prior to its provision to us
perturbation.changed_text: that these terms shall not apply where the Confidential Information: 7.1 was known to us, regardless of how such knowledge was obtained.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the qualification that prior knowledge must not involve a breach of confidentiality significantly alters the scope of the exception. It implies that even if the information was obtained through illegal or unethical means, the confidentiality obligations would not apply, which is against public policy and legal principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Confidence under English Law
perturbation.law_citation: Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the protection of confidential information by suggesting that the source of prior knowledge is irrelevant, even if it involves a breach of confidence, directly contradicting the legal principles established in Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd regarding the duty of confidence. This case establishes the elements of a breach of confidence, including the need for the information to have the necessary quality of confidence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: intellectual property | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you intellectual property Intellectual property (I.P. or IP) is a type of property encompassing the products of original human thought. Common examples of intellectual property include: the contents of a book, designs of an invention, computer software, company logos, and music. Intellectual property differs from other forms of property in that it is non-tangible, and non-rivalrous. Unlike other forms of property, intellectual property can be used by infinitely many people without depriving the original owner of the use of their property. This characteristic of intellectual property makes it particularly vulnerable to free rider problems, creating unique challenges for authors and inventors who wish to monetize their work. Intellectual property rights were created to ensure that authors and inventors are compensated for their efforts to incentivize the production of further works for the benefit of the public. They combat the free rider problem by giving intellectual property owners a near monopoly over the use of their creations for a set period of time. This allows intellectual property owners to license their intellectual property, and to recover monetarily from those who infringe on their intellectual property rights through piracy or wrongful use. However, intellectual property rights are not absolute, and are subject to a myriad of exceptions and affirmative defenses , such as the fair use doctrine. Within the broad category of intellectual property, there are four subcategories of intellectual property: patent , copyright , trademark , and trade secrets . In the United States, patents and copyright are regulated exclusively by federal law, as outlined in the Intellectual Property Clause . Trademarks are areas of shared jurisdiction between federal and state governments, with the federal government deriving their power to regulate trademarks through the Commerce Clause . Trade secrets are largely regulated by states through unfair competition laws. [Last reviewed in June of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords property PROPERTY RIGHTS intellectual property U.S. CONSTITUTION constitutional law Wex CIVICS the Constitution COMMERCE commercial activities PROPERTY business law wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions constitutional law intellectual property copyright law patent law trade secrets law trademark law property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the confidentiality exception only applies if prior knowledge was obtained without a breach of confidentiality. The changed text removes this restriction, implying that even if the information was obtained illegally, the confidentiality obligations would not apply. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that it contradicts legal principles established in Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd, regarding the duty of confidence. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses intellectual property rights and the importance of protecting them, which is undermined by allowing confidential information obtained through illegal means to be exempt from confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: EDT (Electronic document transmissions) Emails with Original Signatures (not electronic) shall be deemed valid and enforceable in respect of any provisions of this NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement). EDT documents shall be subject to European Community Directive No. 95/46/EEC, as applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: EDT (Electronic document transmissions) Emails, regardless of signatures, shall be deemed valid and enforceable. EDT documents are exempt from any data protection regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that EDT documents are exempt from any data protection regulations, directly contradicts the European Community Directive No. 95/46/EEC (now superseded by GDPR) and other data protection laws that apply to electronic data processing, including email communications.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and its predecessor Directive 95/46/EC
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts GDPR (which replaced Directive 95/46/EC) by explicitly stating an exemption from data protection regulations. All processing of personal data, including through EDT, is subject to GDPR requirements regarding lawful basis, data security, and individual rights. The modified text creates a legal conflict.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) – Legal Text Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Welcome to gdpr-info.eu. OJ L 127, 23.5.2018 as a neatly arranged website. All Articles of the GDPR are linked with suitable recitals. The European Data Protection Regulation is applicable as of May 25th, 2018 in all member states to harmonize data privacy laws across Europe. perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly exempts EDT documents from any data protection regulations, which directly contradicts the GDPR's requirement that all processing of personal data is subject to data protection laws. The scraped law snippet 2 states that 'The European Data Protection Regulation is applicable as of May 25th, 2018 in all member states to harmonize data privacy laws across Europe.' Therefore, stating that EDT documents are exempt is a direct contradiction and would likely fail legal scrutiny.
